justification: All 7 business rules identified (BR-1 through BR-7) with correct invariant classifications: accrual rate (false), negative balance (true), 3-day advance notice (false), medical retroactive (false), rejected leave balance integrity (true), contract end date boundary (true), carryover cap (false). Multiple ambiguities surfaced (partial month accrual, calendar vs working days for 5-day window, balance reservation, holiday handling, etc.). 13 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 4. Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated given the number of unspecified edge cases. Bird clearly distinguishes structural domain invariants from configurable HR policies throughout the invariant_justification fields.

observations.text: All 7 rules correctly identified with accurate invariant classification — no misclassifications
Invariant justifications are exceptionally well-reasoned, explaining *why* each rule is or isn't an invariant (e.g., negative balance as ledger corruption vs accrual rate as configurable policy)
13 acceptance criteria with clear Given/When/Then structure, covering accrual, negative balance, advance notice, medical leave exception, rejection balance integrity, contract boundary, and carryover
10 edge cases identified, many of which surface genuine ambiguities (partial month accrual, pending balance reservation, year-spanning leave carryover)
Explicitly surfaces the '5-day window: calendar days or working days?' ambiguity called out in the rubric
Escalations include concrete options and recommendations, not just flag-raising
Confidence calibration at 72 is appropriate — correctly identifies high-confidence areas (core rules) vs low-confidence areas (edge cases)
Business impact analysis adds depth beyond what the rubric requires, covering financial, operational, and compliance dimensions
